rationale_for_baseline_tier: This is a benign, self-limiting mechanical overuse injury with all serious mimics screened negative (no trauma, no night/rest pain, preserved plantarflexion, no heat/redness/swelling or fever, no neuropathic features). The correct disposition is self-care plus a routine podiatry/physiotherapy review within 1-2 weeks and clear safety-netting — tier T1. The source note's 'urgent after-hours, MBS 585-600' framing is a billing/visit justification, not a reflection of true clinical acuity; escalating this presentation to urgent care, ED, or an ambulance would be over-triage.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sudden inability to push the foot down or rise onto the toes, a felt pop or snap, or the heel/ankle suddenly giving way
escalation_edges.rationale: These features suggest an acute Achilles tendon rupture, which needs prompt in-person orthopaedic assessment and immobilisation rather than fasciitis self-care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you suddenly can't point your foot down or push off, feel a pop or snap, or your ankle gives way, get seen urgently the same day (urgent care or your GP) for an assessment of your Achilles tendon.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The heel becomes hot, red, and swollen, or you develop a fever
escalation_edges.rationale: Heat, redness, swelling or fever suggest a soft-tissue or bone infection, which needs urgent same-day assessment and possibly antibiotics.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your heel becomes hot, red and swollen, or you get a fever, see a doctor urgently the same day — this could be an infection rather than the strain.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New numbness, loss of feeling, or persistent pins-and-needles in the foot
escalation_edges.rationale: New sensory loss points to nerve involvement (e.g. tarsal tunnel or entrapment) rather than mechanical fasciitis and needs prompt reassessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop new numbness or loss of feeling in your foot, get reviewed promptly — that points to a nerve problem rather than the heel strain.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The pain changes to a constant, deep, throbbing ache that is present at rest and wakes you from sleep
escalation_edges.rationale: Rest and night pain are atypical for mechanical fasciitis and raise concern for an evolving calcaneal stress fracture, warranting a prompt GP review and possible imaging.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the pain becomes a constant deep ache that is there at rest and wakes you at night, book a prompt GP review within a day or two — this may need an X-ray to check for a stress fracture.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: No meaningful improvement after about two weeks of consistent self-care
escalation_edges.rationale: Persistent symptoms despite conservative measures warrant the planned podiatry/physiotherapy review for biomechanical assessment and orthotic consideration.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the heel is no better after a couple of weeks of the stretches and shoe changes, keep your podiatry or physiotherapy appointment for a biomechanical assessment and possible orthotics.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone with currently restricted mobility because of acute weight-bearing pain
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Confirm the patient can safely mobilise at home, obtain supportive footwear and simple analgesia, and reach help if a red flag appears. Offer a telehealth re-contact if pain or function does not improve, given there is no co-resident to assist.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This looks like a mechanical heel strain that is not dangerous. Start the self-care now — regular ibuprofen with food (if you have no stomach, kidney, or asthma reasons to avoid it) and/or regular paracetamol, the pre-first-step towel stretch, daily calf and foot stretches, evening frozen-bottle rolling, supportive cushioned shoes indoors, and no barefoot walking on hard floors. Book a podiatrist or physiotherapist within 1-2 weeks. Watch for the warning signs below.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Routine GP or allied-health (podiatry/physiotherapy) appointment within 1-2 weeks; telehealth re-contact if not improving
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If a red-flag change appears after hours — a hot, red, swollen heel or fever; new numbness; or a sudden inability to point the foot down — seek urgent same-day care or go to an emergency department. Otherwise the routine appointment in normal hours is appropriate.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Presumptive plantar fasciitis (acute exacerbation) diagnosed on classic first-step morning heel pain; serious mimics screened negative on history. Advise clinician to confirm point tenderness at the medial calcaneal tubercle, the windlass test, and a Thompson test in person, and to consider imaging only if atypical, refractory, or red-flag features develop.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Giving pure self-care with no red-flag safety-net and no referral, such that a disclosed Achilles rupture, infection, or evolving stress fracture would go unescalated.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A missed Achilles rupture or occult stress fracture managed as simple fasciitis leads to tendon retraction or fracture progression and a worse functional outcome.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Escalating a benign mechanical heel injury with all mimics screened negative to urgent care, the emergency department, or an ambulance — causing unnecessary ED attendance, low-value acute imaging, cost and delay, and diversion of urgent resources away from genuine emergencies.
